title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a structural engineer called Oliver Grant talking about the future of eco-friendly skyscrapers. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a structural engineer called Oliver Grant talking about the future of eco-friendly skyscrapers. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Oliver Grant discusses the materials and energy systems used in modern high-rise buildings.
extracts.audio_script:
Hello, I’m Oliver. As the world’s population moves into cities, we need to build up, not out. But traditional skyscrapers are environmental disasters—they consume massive amounts of energy and produce huge amounts of waste. I’m a structural engineer. My interest in sustainable design didn't start with a blueprint; it actually began when I saw a [7]termite mound[/7] during a trip to Australia. I was amazed that these tiny insects could build a structure that maintains a constant temperature without any electricity. I wanted to apply those principles to human architecture.

The biggest carbon cost of a building is the concrete. I’ve found that [11]recycled steel[/11] and engineered timber are the best alternatives. We are now building 'plyscrapers'—tall buildings made almost entirely of wood. It sounds dangerous, but this timber is treated to be as fire-resistant as steel, and it actually stores carbon instead of producing it. It’s a total shift in how we think about construction materials.

Energy efficiency is our next priority. We are designing buildings that 'breathe'. I noticed that [8]natural ventilation[/8] systems—inspired by those termite mounds—can reduce the need for air conditioning by up to 40%. By using the natural rise of hot air, we can create a constant flow of fresh air through the building. It’s better for the environment and better for the health of the people who work inside.

We are also turning the exterior of buildings into power plants. I’ve found that [9]transparent solar panels[/9] are the most exciting development in this area. They look like regular glass, but they capture energy from the sun. Imagine a skyscraper where every window is generating electricity. It would allow the building to be completely self-sufficient, and perhaps even provide energy back to the city grid.

Water management is also critical. I noticed that [10]rainwater harvesting[/10] systems are now being built into the foundations of most new skyscrapers. We collect the water from the roof and the facade, filter it, and use it for flushing toilets and watering the building’s gardens. It’s about creating a closed-loop system where nothing is wasted.

However, there are challenges. I’m worried that [12]government regulations[/12] are not keeping up with the speed of innovation. In many cities, old building codes make it illegal to use the new materials or systems we are developing. We need politicians to be as brave as the engineers if we are going to solve the climate crisis.

Looking forward, I believe we will see 'living facades'. I noticed that [13]algae-filled panels[/13] can be used to grow fuel and provide shade at the same time. The algae absorb CO2 and produce heat that can be used to warm the building's water. It’s a beautiful example of how biology and engineering can work together.

Finally, I’m currently developing a [14]software tool[/14] that helps architects calculate the total environmental impact of their designs in seconds. I want to make sustainability the default choice, not an afterthought. Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: oliver

questions.question: Oliver’s interest in sustainable buildings was inspired by a (7) he saw in the wild.
questions.explanation: He mentions it 'began when I saw a termite mound during a trip to Australia'.

questions.question: Oliver explains that (8) can significantly decrease the amount of power used for cooling.
questions.explanation: He noticed that 'natural ventilation systems... can reduce the need for air conditioning'.

questions.question: According to Oliver, (9) could turn every window in a skyscraper into an energy source.
questions.explanation: He's found that 'transparent solar panels are the most exciting development'.

questions.question: Oliver says that (10) are becoming a standard feature in the base of new buildings.
questions.explanation: He noticed that 'rainwater harvesting systems are now being built into the foundations'.

questions.question: Oliver points out that (11) is a more sustainable choice than traditional building materials.
questions.explanation: He's found that 'recycled steel and engineered timber are the best alternatives'.

questions.question: Oliver is concerned that outdated (12) are slowing down the progress of eco-friendly design.
questions.explanation: He is worried that 'government regulations are not keeping up with the speed of innovation'.

questions.question: Oliver describes how (13) can both generate heat and provide shade for a building.
questions.explanation: He noticed that 'algae-filled panels can be used to grow fuel and provide shade'.

questions.question: Oliver is in the middle of creating a (14) to help other professionals improve their work.
questions.explanation: He is 'currently developing a software tool that helps architects'.
